questions.question: Under the shared responsibility model, who is ALWAYS responsible for the security of customer data, regardless of the cloud service model (IaaS, PaaS, or SaaS)?
questions.explanation: In every cloud service model, the customer always retains responsibility for their data and the identities/accounts that access it. Responsibility for the underlying physical infrastructure, hypervisor, OS, and applications shifts to the provider as you move from IaaS toward SaaS, but data and identity remain the customer's duty.

questions.question: A company is choosing between deploying a workload on Azure Virtual Machines (IaaS) or Microsoft 365 SaaS. In which model does the customer have the GREATEST responsibility for OS patching, network controls, and runtime security?
options: Responsibility is identical across all models
questions.explanation: In IaaS, the provider handles the physical data center, network, and hypervisor; the customer is responsible for the guest OS, runtime, applications, and data — including patching. SaaS shifts almost everything to the provider; the customer manages only data and identities.

questions.question: Which of the following are ALWAYS the customer's responsibility in any cloud service model? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS the customer always owns its information/data and the accounts/identities used to access cloud resources. Physical data center security and hypervisor patching are always the cloud provider's responsibility.

questions.question: A team is migrating a SQL workload to Azure SQL Database (a PaaS offering). Compared to running SQL Server on an Azure VM, which responsibility is offloaded to Microsoft?
options: Patching the underlying database engine and host OS
Choosing which firewall rules grant access
questions.explanation: Azure SQL Database is PaaS, so Microsoft manages and patches the engine and underlying OS. The customer still defines users/permissions, classifies data, and configures network access (firewall rules, private endpoints).

questions.question: A regulator asks who is accountable for the physical security of the data center hosting an Azure-hosted application. Who is responsible?
options: It depends on the workload type
questions.explanation: In every cloud deployment model, the cloud provider is responsible for the physical security of the data center, including physical access controls, environmental safeguards, and the underlying hardware. This never shifts to the customer.

questions.question: Which statement BEST describes the defense-in-depth strategy?
options: Relying on a single, very strong perimeter firewall to block all threats
Layering multiple, independent security controls so that if one fails, others still protect the asset
Encrypting only the data at rest in the database
Outsourcing all security responsibilities to a third party
questions.explanation: Defense-in-depth uses multiple layers of overlapping controls (physical, identity, perimeter, network, compute, application, data) so a single failure does not compromise the system. A single perimeter is the opposite — a "castle and moat" approach.

questions.question: Within Microsoft's defense-in-depth layered model, which layer is described as the "center" — the asset that all other layers protect?
questions.explanation: Data sits at the center of the defense-in-depth model because protecting information is the ultimate goal of all surrounding layers (physical, identity, perimeter, network, compute, application).

questions.question: A security architect adds antivirus on endpoints, MFA at sign-in, a WAF in front of the web app, and TDE on the database. Which principle is being applied?
questions.explanation: Multiple overlapping controls at different layers — endpoint, identity, application, data — is the textbook definition of defense-in-depth. Zero Trust is a related but distinct model centered on "never trust, always verify."

questions.question: Which three principles define the Zero Trust model?
options: Trust but verify; perimeter security; least privilege
Verify explicitly; use least-privilege access; assume breach
Encrypt everything; never log out users; block all traffic
Defense in depth; shared responsibility; identity perimeter
questions.explanation: Zero Trust is built on three guiding principles: Verify explicitly (always authenticate and authorize from all available data points), Use least-privilege access (just-in-time/just-enough access), and Assume breach (segment, encrypt end-to-end, and use analytics to detect threats).

questions.question: A company removes implicit trust from its corporate network — even devices on the LAN must authenticate, prove device health, and pass risk checks before reaching apps. Which model is this?
questions.explanation: Zero Trust eliminates implicit trust based on network location. Every access request is authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated regardless of whether it originates from inside or outside the corporate network.

questions.question: Which Zero Trust principle is enforced when administrators are granted permissions only for the duration of a specific task and only the permissions required to complete it?
questions.explanation: "Use least-privilege access" — including just-in-time (JIT) and just-enough-access (JEA) approaches — limits user permissions to only what is required, only when required. Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management is a concrete implementation.

questions.question: A security team segments the network into micro-segments, encrypts data end-to-end, and routes telemetry into Sentinel for analytics. Which Zero Trust principle is BEST illustrated?
questions.explanation: "Assume breach" recognizes that compromise is inevitable, so you minimize blast radius (segmentation, end-to-end encryption) and improve detection (analytics, threat intelligence) to find and contain breaches quickly.

questions.question: Which TWO are foundational pillars of the Zero Trust model? (Select two.)
options: Public Wi-Fi access for all employees
questions.explanation: Zero Trust applies controls across six pillars: identities, devices/endpoints, applications, data, infrastructure, and network. Identities and devices are two of these pillars; static allow-lists and unrestricted public Wi-Fi contradict the model.

questions.question: A company encrypts data on disks using AES-256. Both encryption and decryption use the same key. Which type of encryption is this?
questions.explanation: Symmetric encryption uses a single shared key for both encryption and decryption (e.g., AES). Asymmetric encryption uses a public/private key pair. Hashing is one-way and not reversible.

questions.question: Which TWO statements about hashing are TRUE? (Select two.)
options: Hashing is reversible with the correct key
Hashing produces a fixed-length value regardless of input size
A small change in input produces a very different hash
Two different inputs are guaranteed to produce different hashes
questions.explanation: A cryptographic hash is one-way (irreversible) and deterministic, producing a fixed-length digest. The avalanche effect means tiny input changes flip many output bits. Collisions (two inputs → same hash) are not guaranteed to be impossible, but they should be computationally infeasible for a strong hash.

questions.question: A web application stores user passwords. Which approach is MOST secure?
options: Store passwords in plaintext for fast lookup
Encrypt passwords with a symmetric key stored next to the database
Store a salted hash of each password
questions.explanation: Passwords should be salted and hashed using a slow, memory-hard hash function (e.g., bcrypt, Argon2). Salting prevents rainbow-table attacks and ensures identical passwords hash to different values. Reversible storage (plaintext, encryption, base64) lets an attacker recover the original.

questions.question: Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair. Which key is used to encrypt a message that ONLY the intended recipient can read?
questions.explanation: To send a confidential message, encrypt with the recipient's PUBLIC key; only the holder of the matching PRIVATE key can decrypt it. To prove origin, the sender encrypts a digest with their OWN private key (a digital signature).

questions.question: Which protection is provided when a TLS connection encrypts traffic between a browser and a web server?
questions.explanation: TLS protects data flowing between two endpoints — this is encryption in transit (in motion). Encryption at rest protects data stored on disk (e.g., Storage Service Encryption, TDE). Both are part of a complete data protection strategy.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the "G" in GRC?
options: Granular access control — fine-grained permissions on every resource
Governance — the framework of policies and processes used to manage and control an organization
Geographic redundancy — replicating data across regions
questions.explanation: In GRC, Governance refers to the system of rules, policies, processes, and roles used to direct and control an organization to meet its objectives. Risk and Compliance complete the model — managing uncertainty and adhering to laws/standards.

questions.question: Match the GRC component to its description: Risk management.
options: Aligning IT decisions to business strategy
Identifying, assessing, and treating events that could prevent goals from being met
Adhering to laws, regulations, and internal policies
Encrypting data at rest and in transit
questions.explanation: Risk management is the discipline of identifying potential events (threats), assessing their impact and likelihood, and applying treatments (avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept). Governance sets direction; compliance focuses on adherence to rules.

questions.question: A company is preparing to demonstrate adherence to ISO 27001 and GDPR. Which GRC component is this work MOST directly part of?
questions.explanation: Compliance addresses adherence to external laws, regulations, and standards (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA, ISO 27001) as well as internal policies. Microsoft Purview Compliance Manager helps assess and improve compliance posture.

questions.question: A company moves most workloads to SaaS. Employees access apps from personal devices on public networks. Which statement BEST justifies the idea that "identity is the new security perimeter"?
options: The corporate firewall now inspects 100% of traffic
Users, apps, and data are outside the traditional network, so identity is the consistent control plane
IP allow-lists are sufficient for all access
Encryption alone makes a network perimeter unnecessary
questions.explanation: When users and apps live outside the corporate LAN, the network perimeter no longer reliably gates access. Identity becomes the consistent control plane — every access decision is anchored to who is requesting, from what device, under what risk.

questions.question: Which scenario BEST illustrates "identity as the primary security perimeter"?
options: Blocking all traffic at a hardware firewall
Requiring MFA and Conditional Access before granting access to cloud apps from any network
Disconnecting the office from the internet
questions.explanation: Using identity-aware controls (MFA, Conditional Access based on user/device/location/risk) to gate every access request — regardless of network origin — is the canonical example of identity-as-perimeter.

questions.question: A company moves from a flat on-premises Active Directory model to a cloud-first Microsoft Entra ID deployment. Why does identity become MORE important in this shift?
options: Identity becomes the single, consistent boundary that protects resources accessed from anywhere
Encryption replaces the need for identity controls
Cloud apps do not require sign-in
questions.explanation: In cloud-first deployments, users, devices, and apps connect from everywhere. A robust identity layer becomes the single consistent control plane that authenticates and authorizes every access request.

questions.question: Which statement BEST defines authentication?
options: The process of determining what an identity is allowed to do
The process of proving that an identity is who it claims to be
Logging which user accessed which resource
questions.explanation: Authentication (AuthN) verifies an identity's claim — typically via something you know (password), have (token), or are (biometric). Authorization (AuthZ) — a separate step — decides what that authenticated identity can do.

questions.question: A user enters a password AND approves a push notification on their phone. This is an example of what?
questions.explanation: Combining two or more independent factors (something you know + something you have, etc.) is multifactor authentication. A push notification approved on a registered device counts as a "have" factor.

questions.question: Single sign-on (SSO) achieves which outcome?
options: Users authenticate once and access multiple connected applications without re-entering credentials
Users must sign in separately to every application
Eliminates the need for any authentication
questions.explanation: SSO lets a user authenticate one time and access many connected apps without re-prompting. It works alongside MFA — MFA strengthens the initial sign-in event that grants the SSO session.

questions.question: Which statement BEST defines authorization?
options: Determining what an authenticated identity is permitted to do
questions.explanation: Authorization (AuthZ) follows authentication. After an identity has proven itself, authorization decides which resources and actions are allowed — typically via roles, group memberships, and policies.

questions.question: A developer asks: "Is the user signed in?" vs. "Can the user delete this record?" Which two concepts do these questions map to, respectively?
questions.explanation: "Is the user signed in?" is an authentication question. "Can the user perform this action?" is an authorization question. The two are distinct steps in any access decision.

questions.question: In role-based access control (RBAC), what determines which actions a user is allowed to perform?
options: The roles assigned to the user (or to groups they belong to)
The strength of the user's password
questions.explanation: RBAC grants permissions by assigning users (or groups, service principals, managed identities) to roles. Each role contains a set of allowed actions on a scope. The assigned roles determine what an authenticated identity can do.

questions.question: Which statement BEST describes an identity provider (IdP)?
options: A service that stores and manages digital identities and authenticates users on behalf of applications
A service that encrypts data at rest
A network firewall that filters traffic
A directory service that stores only physical assets
questions.explanation: An identity provider (IdP) is a system that creates, stores, and manages digital identities and authenticates users/services to applications. Microsoft Entra ID, Google, Facebook, and on-premises Active Directory Federation Services are examples.

questions.question: A user signs in to a third-party app using their Microsoft work account. Microsoft Entra ID returns a token confirming the user's identity. In this flow, Microsoft Entra ID is acting as what?
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra ID acts as the identity provider — it authenticates the user and issues a token. The third-party application is the relying party that trusts and consumes the token.

questions.question: Which of the following are common identity providers? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra ID and Google Identity are identity providers — they authenticate users and issue tokens. Azure Storage stores blobs; Sentinel is a SIEM/SOAR. Neither serves as an IdP.

questions.question: A SaaS app is configured to trust Microsoft Entra ID tokens issued for several customer tenants. This pattern is an example of what?
questions.explanation: When two identity domains establish trust so a token from one is accepted in the other, that's federation. SAML and OpenID Connect are common federation protocols. SaaS apps frequently federate with multiple customer IdPs.

questions.question: Why is centralizing authentication through an IdP a security improvement over each app maintaining its own user database?
options: It increases attack surface and reduces visibility
It enables consistent policy enforcement (MFA, Conditional Access) and centralized auditing
It eliminates the need for any authentication
It removes the need for authorization
questions.explanation: Centralized IdPs allow a single place to enforce sign-in policies (MFA, risk-based Conditional Access), monitor sign-ins, and disable compromised accounts. Per-app credential silos increase risk and reduce visibility.

questions.question: What is a directory service?
options: A specialized database that stores information about objects (users, groups, devices) and is optimized for read-heavy lookups
questions.explanation: A directory service stores and organizes information about network objects — users, groups, computers, services — and is optimized for fast lookups. Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) is the on-prem example; Microsoft Entra ID is the cloud directory.

questions.question: Which of the following is the MOST accurate description of on-premises Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS)?
options: An on-premises directory service that uses LDAP and Kerberos for authentication
questions.explanation: AD DS is an on-premises directory service that organizes objects into a hierarchical domain/forest structure and authenticates clients using Kerberos and NTLM. It speaks LDAP for directory queries. Microsoft Entra ID is the cloud equivalent (different protocols).

questions.question: A company uses on-premises AD DS for file servers and apps, and Microsoft Entra ID for SaaS apps. Users sign in once and access both. Which two concepts make this possible? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Hybrid identity tools (Microsoft Entra Connect Sync, cloud sync, password hash sync, pass-through authentication, federation) synchronize/connect AD DS with Entra ID. Combined with SSO, users get a unified sign-in across on-prem and cloud apps.

questions.question: What is federation, in the identity context?
options: A trust relationship between two identity domains so a user authenticated in one can access resources in the other
questions.explanation: Federation establishes trust between identity domains so tokens issued by one IdP can be accepted by relying parties in another. SAML and OpenID Connect are common federation protocols. It enables cross-organization single sign-on.

questions.question: A partner company's users sign in with their own corporate identities to access your SharePoint Online site. Which capability supports this WITHOUT creating accounts in your tenant?
questions.explanation: Federation (or Microsoft Entra B2B collaboration, which builds on federation/external IdP trust) lets external users sign in with their home credentials and access your resources without you creating and managing accounts for them.

questions.question: Which statement BEST describes Microsoft Entra ID?
options: A cloud-based identity and access management service that authenticates users and authorizes access to apps and resources
An on-premises domain controller replacement that uses Kerberos and LDAP
A SIEM platform for collecting security logs
A database service for relational workloads
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is Microsoft's cloud-based identity and access management service. It authenticates users and applications and authorizes access to Microsoft 365, Azure, and thousands of SaaS apps. It is not a direct on-prem AD DS replacement (different protocols).

questions.question: A user signs in to Microsoft 365. Which service authenticates them?
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra ID is the identity provider for Microsoft 365 (and Azure). Every sign-in to a Microsoft 365 service is brokered by Entra ID, which issues the tokens used by Exchange, SharePoint, Teams, etc.

questions.question: Which TWO statements about the differences between Microsoft Entra ID and on-premises Active Directory Domain Services are TRUE? (Select two.)
options: AD DS uses Kerberos/NTLM and LDAP; Entra ID uses SAML, OAuth, and OpenID Connect
Entra ID is a hierarchical, OU-based directory like AD DS
Entra ID is delivered as a cloud service; AD DS is installed on a server (domain controller)
AD DS is the cloud successor to Microsoft Entra ID
questions.explanation: AD DS is an on-prem directory using Kerberos/NTLM and LDAP, organized into OUs and forests. Entra ID is a flat (non-hierarchical) cloud identity service speaking modern protocols (SAML, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect). They complement each other in hybrid scenarios.

questions.question: Which TYPE of identity in Microsoft Entra ID represents a non-human application or service that needs access to resources?
questions.explanation: A service principal is the Entra ID identity for an application or workload — used so the app can authenticate and be authorized just like a user. Managed identities are a special, automatically managed form of service principal.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra ID identity type AUTOMATICALLY manages credentials for an Azure resource (such as a VM or Function App)?
questions.explanation: A managed identity is automatically provisioned by Azure for a resource (system-assigned) or created and shared between resources (user-assigned). Credentials are rotated and never exposed to the developer — eliminating secret management.

questions.question: What is the difference between a system-assigned managed identity and a user-assigned managed identity?
options: System-assigned identities can be reused across multiple resources; user-assigned cannot
System-assigned identities are tied to a single resource and deleted with it; user-assigned identities are standalone and can be shared across resources
User-assigned identities are only available for SaaS apps
questions.explanation: A system-assigned managed identity's lifecycle is tied to its host resource — deleted with it. A user-assigned managed identity is a standalone Azure resource that can be assigned to one or many resources, persisting independently.

questions.question: Which kind of Microsoft Entra account is created so a partner organization's user can access your resources using their existing credentials?
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra B2B collaboration adds external users as guests in your tenant. They sign in with their home identity (no separate password in your tenant) and you authorize them like any other user.

questions.question: A customer-facing application uses Microsoft Entra to authenticate its end consumers. Which Entra capability is purpose-built for this scenario?
options: Microsoft Entra External ID for customers (CIAM)
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra External ID (the modern CIAM offering, succeeding Azure AD B2C in many scenarios) is designed for customer/consumer identity — sign-up, sign-in, and identity flows for external app users at scale.

questions.question: Which TWO Microsoft Entra ID concepts can be used to define a logical container for users and groups so that admin permissions can be DELEGATED to a subset of the directory?
options: Scoped role assignments via administrative units
questions.explanation: Administrative units (AUs) let you scope admin roles to a subset of users/groups (e.g., a region or business unit). Combined with scoped role assignments, AUs delegate management without granting tenant-wide privilege.

questions.question: Which statement BEST describes hybrid identity?
options: Identities exist only in the cloud
A common identity is used across on-premises and cloud, typically by synchronizing or federating an on-prem AD with Microsoft Entra ID
Each user has two unrelated identities
questions.explanation: Hybrid identity provides a single user identity usable across on-prem and cloud apps. Microsoft Entra Connect Sync or cloud sync replicates accounts from AD DS to Entra ID; password hash sync, pass-through auth, or federation handle sign-in.

questions.question: Which Microsoft tool is the recommended primary method to synchronize on-premises Active Directory objects to Microsoft Entra ID?
options: Microsoft Entra Connect (Sync or cloud sync)
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra Connect (Sync) — and the lighter Microsoft Entra cloud sync — are the supported tools for synchronizing on-premises AD users, groups, and (optionally) credential hashes into Microsoft Entra ID.

questions.question: Which authentication method lets users sign in to Microsoft Entra ID with the SAME password as on-premises AD by synchronizing a hash of the hashed password to the cloud?
questions.explanation: PHS synchronizes a hash of the on-prem password hash to Entra ID, allowing cloud sign-in with the same password. PTA validates passwords against on-prem AD in real time without storing hashes. Federation hands off authentication to AD FS or another IdP.

questions.question: A company wants on-premises AD to remain the authoritative source for password validation, with passwords NEVER stored in any form in the cloud. Which hybrid auth method is the BEST fit?
questions.explanation: Pass-through authentication (PTA) sends sign-in attempts to a lightweight agent that validates them against on-prem AD in real time. No password material is stored in the cloud directory.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra concept represents a dedicated, isolated instance of Entra ID used by an organization?
questions.explanation: A Microsoft Entra tenant is a dedicated directory instance an organization receives when it signs up for a Microsoft cloud service. Subscriptions, resource groups, and management groups are Azure billing/governance constructs that trust a tenant.

questions.question: Which of the following are objects stored in Microsoft Entra ID? (Select three.)
questions.explanation: Entra ID stores users (members and guests), groups (security and Microsoft 365), and applications (along with service principals representing them). Subnets are Azure network constructs, not directory objects.

questions.question: What is a Microsoft Entra "Workload identity"?
options: An identity used by a software workload (application, service, script) — including service principals and managed identities — to access resources
A user identity restricted to weekdays
A type of phone-based MFA factor
questions.explanation: A workload identity represents a non-human principal — applications, services, scripts, automation. In Entra ID this is typically a service principal or a managed identity. Workload identity protection capabilities (e.g., risk detections) are also available.

questions.question: Microsoft Entra Domain Services provides which capability?
options: A managed domain-joined service offering Kerberos, NTLM, LDAP, and group policy in Azure — without you running domain controllers
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra Domain Services (formerly Azure AD DS) provides a managed AD-DS-compatible domain in Azure: classic Kerberos/NTLM/LDAP and group policy for legacy apps and VMs — without you managing domain controllers.

questions.question: Which TWO advantages does Microsoft Entra ID provide over per-app local user databases? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Entra ID centralizes identity management — provisioning, deprovisioning, group-based access, audit — and lets you enforce SSO and MFA consistently across apps. It does not replace backup nor exempt you from compliance.

questions.question: In Microsoft Entra ID, who is responsible for assigning licenses to users so they can use Microsoft 365 features?
options: A Microsoft Entra administrator (e.g., License Administrator or Global Admin)
questions.explanation: Licenses are assigned by an administrator (directly or via group-based licensing). Common roles include License Administrator and Global Administrator. End users typically cannot self-assign paid licenses.

questions.question: A developer wants an Azure Function to call Azure Key Vault WITHOUT storing any secrets in code or configuration. Which Microsoft Entra capability should they use?
questions.explanation: A managed identity gives the Function App an Entra ID identity whose credentials are managed and rotated by Azure. The function uses it to obtain tokens for Key Vault — no secrets in code or app settings.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid authentication methods supported by Microsoft Entra ID? (Select three.)
options: Reading the password aloud over the phone
questions.explanation: Entra ID supports a range of methods: passwords, Authenticator app (push, OTP, passwordless), FIDO2 security keys, Windows Hello for Business, certificate-based authentication, SMS/voice, and OATH tokens. Speaking a password is not an authentication method.

questions.question: A user enrolls a FIDO2 security key for sign-in. Which characteristic BEST describes FIDO2?
options: It is a passwordless, phishing-resistant authentication method based on public-key cryptography
It transmits a shared secret over the network
questions.explanation: FIDO2 / WebAuthn is a passwordless, phishing-resistant standard. The key holds a private key bound to the device and a domain; only a signed challenge is sent — no shareable secret traverses the network.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra capability requires a user to present TWO or more forms of evidence at sign-in?
questions.explanation: Multifactor authentication requires two or more factors — typically something you know (password) plus something you have (Authenticator/security key) or something you are (biometric). It is one of the highest-impact controls against account compromise.

questions.question: Which TWO authentication methods are considered passwordless in Microsoft Entra ID? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Windows Hello for Business and FIDO2 security keys (and the Authenticator app's passwordless sign-in) are passwordless methods. SMS one-time passcode with a password is still a password-based method augmented with a second factor.

questions.question: A company wants users to be able to reset their own forgotten passwords without calling the help desk. Which Microsoft Entra feature provides this?
questions.explanation: SSPR lets users reset/change passwords on their own after verifying with authentication methods they have registered (e.g., phone, email, security questions, Authenticator). It can be combined with password writeback to update on-prem AD.

questions.question: Combined registration in Microsoft Entra ID lets users register information for which TWO capabilities at the same time? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Combined security info registration enrolls users for both MFA and SSPR in one experience, reducing friction and ensuring both capabilities have the methods needed.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra password protection feature helps prevent users from choosing weak or commonly attacked passwords?
options: Microsoft Entra Password Protection (global and custom banned password lists)
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra Password Protection enforces a global banned password list (Microsoft-curated) and an optional custom list. It evaluates new and changed passwords (cloud and, with the agent, on-prem AD) against fuzzy matches and substitutions.

questions.question: How does Microsoft Entra Password Protection evaluate a candidate password?
options: Fuzzy matching that accounts for character substitutions (e.g., "P@ssw0rd!" → "password")
It does not block any passwords
It encrypts the password and stores it
questions.explanation: Password Protection performs fuzzy/normalized matching that detects common substitutions (P@ssw0rd, P4ssword) and substring matches against the banned list, blocking near-matches not just exact strings.

questions.question: Smart lockout in Microsoft Entra ID protects against what?
options: Brute-force password guessing attacks against user accounts
Cross-site scripting attacks on web apps
questions.explanation: Smart lockout tracks failed sign-ins per account and locks the account temporarily after repeated bad attempts — while attempting to distinguish legitimate users from attackers so genuine users are not locked out.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra authentication method allows a user to approve a sign-in with a push notification on their registered phone?
questions.explanation: Microsoft Authenticator provides push notifications, time-based one-time passcodes (TOTP), and passwordless phone sign-in. It is Microsoft's recommended app-based MFA method.

questions.question: A bank requires sign-in with a smart card from corporate-issued devices. Which Microsoft Entra authentication method supports this?
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra certificate-based authentication (CBA) lets users authenticate with X.509 client certificates (including smart cards) directly against Entra ID — no federated AD FS required.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra capability blocks a known compromised password from being set, even if it would otherwise meet length and complexity requirements?
questions.explanation: Password Protection blocks passwords matching the global banned list (and optional custom list) regardless of length/complexity, preventing users from setting widely compromised or commonly targeted strings.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra authentication method is generally considered the LEAST secure of the supported MFA factors and is being deprecated for many scenarios?
questions.explanation: SMS is the weakest MFA method (susceptible to SIM swap and SS7 attacks). Microsoft and industry guidance recommend phishing-resistant methods like FIDO2, Windows Hello, and Authenticator passwordless.

questions.question: What is Number Matching in Microsoft Authenticator?
options: A feature that displays a number on the sign-in screen which the user must enter in the Authenticator app to approve the sign-in, mitigating MFA fatigue attacks
A way to dial international numbers automatically
A method to count failed sign-ins
questions.explanation: Number matching strengthens push-based MFA: the user must read a number from the sign-in screen and type it into the Authenticator app. This defeats MFA-fatigue ("push bombing") attacks that rely on a user reflexively approving a push.

questions.question: A company wants to allow temporary Microsoft Entra access for a contractor who has no permanent phone. Which Microsoft Entra mechanism issues a time-limited passcode for initial sign-in or recovery?
questions.explanation: A Temporary Access Pass is a time-limited passcode issued by an admin (or via SSPR for self-service) used to onboard, register passwordless methods, or recover from MFA registration loss.

questions.question: Which TWO statements about Microsoft Entra MFA are TRUE? (Select two.)
options: MFA can be enforced via Conditional Access policies
MFA always requires SMS as a factor
MFA dramatically reduces the success of credential-theft attacks
MFA is only available in on-premises AD DS
questions.explanation: MFA in Entra ID is most flexibly enforced via Conditional Access (allowing risk-based, app-based, location-based policies). Microsoft data shows MFA blocks the vast majority of automated account-compromise attempts. MFA supports many factors — SMS is not required and is discouraged.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra feature enables a user to register for SSPR and MFA at the same time during onboarding?
questions.explanation: Combined registration gives users a single experience for registering authentication methods used by both MFA and SSPR — improving adoption and reducing helpdesk calls.

questions.question: Authentication strength in Microsoft Entra Conditional Access lets administrators do what?
options: Require specific authentication methods (e.g., only phishing-resistant MFA like FIDO2) for sensitive apps
questions.explanation: Authentication strength is a Conditional Access control that specifies WHICH methods satisfy MFA for a given policy — for example, requiring phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2 key, Windows Hello, certificate) when accessing high-value apps.

questions.question: A user authenticates with a security question. Which Microsoft Entra capability ONLY (not for sign-in to apps) is this method typically used for?
questions.explanation: Security questions are a weak factor and in Microsoft Entra ID are usable only for self-service password reset — never as a sign-in or MFA factor for cloud apps.

questions.question: Why is requiring MFA for ALL administrators considered a high-priority security baseline?
options: Admins' credentials are high-value targets and compromise can pivot to full tenant takeover
Admins use weaker passwords than other users
MFA reduces admin productivity by design
MFA is only for admins, not regular users
questions.explanation: Privileged accounts are prime targets — compromise can grant tenant-wide control. Enforcing MFA (preferably phishing-resistant MFA) on every privileged role is the single highest-leverage identity control and a baseline in security defaults.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra feature evaluates signals (user, device, location, app, risk) at sign-in and applies controls such as MFA, compliant device required, or block?
questions.explanation: Conditional Access is Entra ID's policy engine that combines signals into "if-then" decisions: if assignments (users, apps, conditions) match, apply access controls (grant with MFA, require compliant device, block, etc.).

questions.question: The "if-then" form of a Conditional Access policy is BEST described as:
options: "If a user is assigned to a role, then grant the role"
"If certain assignments (signals) are met, then apply specific access controls"
"If an application has high CPU, then scale out"
"If a user has admin rights, then encrypt their drive"
questions.explanation: A Conditional Access policy is structured as Assignments (who/what/conditions) → Access controls (grant/block, and any required controls). Conceptually: if the signals match, then enforce these controls.

questions.question: Which TWO of the following can be configured as signals in a Conditional Access policy? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Conditional Access uses assignments such as users/groups, applications, conditions (sign-in risk, user risk, device platform, location, client app). Disk size and antivirus brand are not direct Conditional Access signals.

questions.question: A company wants to require MFA only when users sign in from outside corporate locations. Which Conditional Access condition supports this?
questions.explanation: Named locations let admins define trusted IP ranges or countries. A Conditional Access policy can then require MFA when the location is NOT one of these trusted locations.

questions.question: Role-based access control (RBAC) in Microsoft Entra ID is BEST described as:
options: A model where permissions are granted by assigning users (or groups, service principals) to predefined or custom roles at a defined scope
A way to encrypt data at rest
A method of password complexity enforcement
questions.explanation: RBAC assigns roles (collections of allowed actions) to security principals (users, groups, service principals) at a specific scope. Microsoft Entra has its own roles for directory administration; Azure RBAC controls Azure resource permissions.

questions.question: Which TWO of these are valid scopes for an Azure RBAC role assignment? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Azure RBAC role assignments can be applied at the management group, subscription, resource group, or individual resource scope. Tenant region and subnet IPs are not RBAC scopes.

questions.question: Microsoft Entra built-in directory roles (e.g., Global Administrator, User Administrator) control what?
options: Permissions over Microsoft Entra directory objects and certain Microsoft 365 services
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra built-in roles (e.g., Global Administrator, User Administrator, Application Administrator) grant permissions over directory data and many Microsoft 365 admin actions. Azure resource permissions are governed by separate Azure RBAC roles.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra role provides the BROADEST set of privileges across the tenant, including all administrative features?
questions.explanation: Global Administrator has unrestricted access to all Entra administrative features and many connected services. Microsoft recommends keeping the number of Global Admins very small and protecting them with phishing-resistant MFA and PIM.

questions.question: Why is it considered a best practice to LIMIT the number of permanent Global Administrator assignments?
options: Global Admins have wide privileges; reducing standing access limits blast radius of compromise
Global Admins cost more in licensing
questions.explanation: A Global Admin can perform virtually any administrative action; a compromised Global Admin can take over the tenant. Minimizing permanent assignments — and using just-in-time activation via PIM — reduces this risk.

questions.question: A custom Conditional Access policy targeting all users for "Require MFA" would typically EXCLUDE which account?
questions.explanation: Best practice is to exclude one or two highly secured "break-glass" / emergency access accounts from Conditional Access (and most blocking policies) so admins can recover if MFA infrastructure or a misconfigured policy locks everyone out.

questions.question: Conditional Access can require a device to be marked "compliant" by which Microsoft service before granting access?
questions.explanation: "Require device to be marked as compliant" relies on Microsoft Intune (or another supported MDM) attesting the device meets compliance policies (encryption, OS version, antivirus, etc.). Hybrid Entra joined or Entra joined is a related signal.

questions.question: Which TWO are valid grant controls in a Conditional Access policy? (Select two.)
options: Force a password reset every sign-in
Require the user to type a CAPTCHA
questions.explanation: Grant controls include: require MFA, require compliant device, require Entra-joined device, require approved client app, require app protection policy, require terms of use, require authentication strength, and combinations. Password reset on every sign-in and CAPTCHA are not Conditional Access grant controls.

questions.question: Which Conditional Access session control limits what a user can DO within an app once granted access (e.g., block downloads from SharePoint Online)?
options: Conditional Access App Control (with Defender for Cloud Apps)
questions.explanation: Conditional Access App Control, integrated with Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps, applies session policies (block download, monitor only, control upload) inline in the user's session for selected cloud apps.

questions.question: A company has a Conditional Access policy "Require MFA for all users" and exempts a service account that runs nightly automation. What's the safer alternative to a CA exclusion in this case?
options: Convert the script to use a managed identity or workload identity with appropriate role assignments
Hardcode the password in the script
Share the service account credentials in email
questions.explanation: Replacing a username/password service account with a managed identity or service principal (using certificate or federated credentials) removes the password risk entirely and eliminates the need for CA exclusions.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra capability lets administrators create custom RBAC roles in Microsoft Entra ID?
options: Custom roles (Microsoft Entra role-based access control)
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra ID supports custom roles for directory administration so organizations can tailor permissions beyond built-in roles. Azure RBAC also supports custom roles for Azure resource permissions.

questions.question: In Conditional Access "Report-only" mode, what happens?
options: The policy is logged as if it were applied but does NOT enforce controls, allowing administrators to evaluate impact before turning it on
The policy applies only to admins
questions.explanation: Report-only mode evaluates the policy and records the result in sign-in logs (and Workbooks) without enforcing controls. Administrators can validate scope and impact before switching the policy On.

questions.question: Which TWO actions can an administrator take on a Conditional Access policy state? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: A Conditional Access policy state can be On, Off, or Report-only. The first two enforce/disable; report-only logs what WOULD happen without affecting users.

questions.question: Which Azure RBAC role is the BEST fit for a user who needs to view all resources but should not be able to make any changes?
questions.explanation: Reader grants view-only access. Contributor allows creating and managing resources but not assigning RBAC. Owner adds RBAC assignment. User Access Administrator manages access only.

questions.question: In Azure RBAC, the Owner role differs from Contributor in which way?
options: Owner can also manage role assignments; Contributor cannot
Owner is read-only; Contributor is full access
questions.explanation: Both can manage Azure resources, but only Owner (and User Access Administrator) can assign Azure RBAC roles to others. Contributor lacks the right to grant access.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra feature lets administrators define WHICH apps and resources a user can request access to AND get the request approved by a designated approver?
options: Microsoft Entra ID Governance — entitlement management (access packages)
questions.explanation: Entitlement management bundles resources (groups, apps, SharePoint sites) into access packages with policies governing who can request, approval flow, and lifetime/recertification — an identity-governance core capability.

questions.question: What is the goal of Microsoft Entra ID Governance?
options: Ensure the right people have the right access to the right resources, for the right reasons, for the right length of time
questions.explanation: Microsoft Entra ID Governance is the umbrella for capabilities that govern identity lifecycle and access: entitlement management, access reviews, PIM, lifecycle workflows, and reporting. The objective is least-privilege, time-bound, justified access.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra capability automates recurring CONFIRMATION that users still need their current access?
questions.explanation: Access reviews periodically prompt reviewers (managers, group owners, or users themselves) to confirm that group membership, app access, or role assignments are still required, and automatically remove access that is denied or unattested.

questions.question: A reviewer in an access review can take which TWO actions on a user's access? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Reviewers decide whether each user's access is still needed and approve (keep) or deny (remove). They cannot edit identities or reset passwords from within the review.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra feature lets administrators be ELIGIBLE for a privileged role and activate it just-in-time with approval, MFA, and a time limit?
questions.explanation: PIM provides just-in-time, time-bound access to Microsoft Entra and Azure roles. Eligible users activate the role on demand — optionally with approval, MFA, and justification — minimizing standing privileged access.

questions.question: Which TWO benefits does Microsoft Entra PIM provide? (Select two.)
options: Eliminates standing privileged access by making roles eligible/activate on demand
Provides an audit trail of role activations and approvals
questions.explanation: PIM removes "always on" admin rights, provides approval workflows, MFA enforcement on activation, and a full audit log of who activated which role, when, and why. It complements but does not replace SIEM/MFA.

questions.question: Microsoft Entra ID Protection detects sign-in risks based on which kind of signals?
options: User and sign-in risk signals derived from Microsoft's threat intelligence (anonymous IP, atypical travel, leaked credentials, etc.)
questions.explanation: ID Protection uses Microsoft's threat intelligence and ML to surface risk detections like atypical travel, anonymous IP, leaked credentials, password spray, malicious IP, and more. Risks are scored at user and sign-in levels.

questions.question: Which TWO actions can be triggered automatically by a Microsoft Entra ID Protection risk-based policy? (Select two.)
options: Require MFA for a risky sign-in
Require password change for a high-risk user
Schedule a backup of an Azure SQL database
questions.explanation: ID Protection (and Conditional Access using ID Protection signals) can require MFA on risky sign-ins and force password change on risky users (often with MFA). It is an identity policy engine — it does not manage Azure resources directly.

questions.question: In Microsoft Entra ID Protection, the difference between "user risk" and "sign-in risk" is BEST described as:
options: User risk = probability the user identity itself is compromised; sign-in risk = probability a specific sign-in is not the legitimate user
User risk only applies to admins; sign-in risk only to end users
Sign-in risk relates to billing fraud
questions.explanation: User risk reflects the likelihood that a user account is compromised (e.g., leaked credentials in dumps). Sign-in risk reflects the likelihood that a particular sign-in event is fraudulent (e.g., atypical travel). Each drives different policies.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra capability automates JOINER/MOVER/LEAVER processes — provisioning, updating, and revoking access as users change roles?
questions.explanation: Lifecycle workflows (part of Microsoft Entra ID Governance) automate tasks tied to user lifecycle events — sending welcome emails, provisioning group/app access on join, revoking access and disabling accounts on leave.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra capability bundles multiple resources into a single REQUESTABLE unit with policies for who can request, approval, and expiration?
questions.explanation: Entitlement management lets admins package resources (groups, apps, SharePoint sites) and define access policies — including approval workflows and access expiration — so users can self-serve while governance is enforced.

questions.question: A user signs in from an anonymous IP address (e.g., Tor exit node). What kind of ID Protection signal is this?
questions.explanation: Anonymous IP is a sign-in-time risk detection raised by ID Protection during the sign-in event. The risk can drive a Conditional Access policy that requires MFA or blocks the sign-in.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra ID Protection detection indicates the user's credentials were found in a known credential leak or breach dump?
questions.explanation: Microsoft's threat research finds credential dumps and matches against tenant accounts. Matches raise the "leaked credentials" detection, flagging high user risk and typically triggering a forced password change with MFA.

questions.question: Which TWO controls help reduce STANDING administrative privilege in Microsoft Entra ID? (Select two.)
options: PIM eligible assignments with just-in-time activation
Granting all admins permanent Global Administrator
Approval requirements and time-bound activation for sensitive roles
questions.explanation: PIM-based eligibility, JIT activation, time-bound access, approval, and MFA all reduce the window during which any admin holds elevated privileges. The opposite — permanent assignments and shared passwords — increases standing risk.

questions.question: Access reviews can be configured to AUTO-APPLY review decisions. What does this mean?
options: Decisions made by reviewers are automatically applied at the end of the review (e.g., removing denied access)
questions.explanation: Auto-apply causes decisions to take effect when the review completes — for example, removing users whose access was denied or not reviewed. Without auto-apply, admins must manually apply outcomes.

questions.question: A user is asked to attest to their OWN need for membership in a group during an access review. This kind of review is called:
questions.explanation: Access reviews support several reviewer types: manager, group/app owner, a designated user, or the users themselves (self-review). Self-reviews can be useful but are typically combined with other controls because users tend to self-approve.

questions.question: What does PIM's "Discovery" insight in Microsoft Entra typically surface?
options: Recommendations such as users with permanent assignments who could be moved to eligible assignments
A list of available Azure regions
A list of installed browser extensions
questions.explanation: PIM provides insights such as identifying privileged users with permanent assignments who should be moved to eligible/JIT and other recommendations for reducing standing privilege.

questions.question: Which TWO are typical OUTPUTS of Microsoft Entra ID Governance? (Select two.)
options: Periodic access reviews of group memberships and role assignments
questions.explanation: Governance produces evidence: access reviews, audit trails of activations and changes, certifications, and entitlement reports. Encryption and networking are separate Azure services.

questions.question: Risk-based Conditional Access policies use ID Protection signals to drive access decisions. Which combination is a COMMON pattern?
options: On HIGH sign-in risk, require MFA; on HIGH user risk, require secure password change
On HIGH sign-in risk, disable the tenant
On any risk, delete the user
Risk policies cannot be combined with Conditional Access
questions.explanation: A common pattern: require MFA on medium/high sign-in risk; require a secure (MFA-protected) password change on medium/high user risk. This automatically remediates the most common compromise scenarios.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra licensing tier is typically required for advanced governance features such as Privileged Identity Management, access reviews, and ID Protection risk policies?
options: Microsoft Entra ID P1/P2 (with PIM, access reviews, and risk policies in P2)
Microsoft Entra Free in all cases
questions.explanation: Many governance and protection features require Microsoft Entra ID P1 or P2 (formerly Azure AD Premium P1/P2). PIM, access reviews, and ID Protection risk-based policies typically require P2 (or equivalent suite licensing).

questions.question: How does PIM minimize the "blast radius" of a compromised admin account?
options: By making the role merely eligible rather than active — so the attacker first needs to activate it (with MFA/approval) before exercising privileges
By disabling the user account whenever they sign in
By replacing the user's password every hour
questions.explanation: With PIM, an attacker who steals credentials still must activate the role — passing MFA, approval, and time-window checks. This narrows the attack window and creates auditable activation events.

questions.question: What does Azure DDoS Protection defend against?
options: Volumetric, protocol, and resource-layer attacks that attempt to overwhelm public-facing resources and make them unavailable
questions.explanation: Azure DDoS Protection mitigates distributed denial-of-service attacks targeting Azure public endpoints — at the volumetric (UDP/ICMP flood), protocol (SYN, fragmentation), and resource (Layer 7) levels. It does not address application logic flaws like SQLi or phishing.

questions.question: Which TWO tiers of Azure DDoS Protection exist? (Select two.)
options: DDoS Network Protection (a paid SKU for VNets)
DDoS IP Protection (a per-public-IP paid option)
questions.explanation: Azure offers paid DDoS Network Protection (covers an entire VNet) and DDoS IP Protection (per public IP). Free, always-on Basic protection is included on the Azure platform for all public IPs. There is no SQL-specific DDoS SKU.

questions.question: Which kind of DDoS attack attempts to consume bandwidth by flooding the target with massive UDP/ICMP traffic?
questions.explanation: Volumetric attacks aim to saturate network bandwidth. Protocol attacks (e.g., SYN floods) target state on firewalls/load balancers. Layer 7 attacks abuse application logic (e.g., HTTP floods). DDoS Protection mitigates all three.

questions.question: Azure DDoS Protection Standard provides which TWO ENHANCEMENTS over the always-on basic platform protection? (Select two.)
options: Adaptive tuning based on application traffic patterns
Cost protection / SLA-backed credits for scale-out during attacks
Encryption of stored data at rest
questions.explanation: DDoS Protection Standard tunes mitigation thresholds based on the protected app's normal traffic, offers attack analytics and DDoS Rapid Response, and provides cost protection — credits for scale-out triggered by an attack.

questions.question: Which Azure resource is the typical TARGET of DDoS Protection coverage?
options: Public IP addresses associated with Azure resources (e.g., Application Gateway, Azure Firewall, VMs with public IPs)
questions.explanation: DDoS Protection covers public IP addresses on Azure resources. Resources with only private IPs (behind a load balancer or firewall) are not directly addressable from the internet.

questions.question: What is Azure Firewall?
options: A managed, cloud-native, stateful network security service that filters traffic to/from Azure VNets
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall is a managed, stateful firewall service offering threat-intelligence-based filtering, FQDN filtering, network/application rules, NAT, and high availability — all without you maintaining firewall appliances.

questions.question: Which TWO rule types does Azure Firewall support? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall supports Network rules (Layer 3/4: source, destination, protocol, port), Application rules (FQDN/URL filtering), and NAT rules (DNAT for inbound). It is not an identity service.

questions.question: Threat intelligence-based filtering in Azure Firewall does what?
options: Alerts on, or blocks, traffic to/from known malicious IP addresses and domains from Microsoft's threat feed
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall can leverage Microsoft Threat Intelligence to alert or alert-and-deny traffic to/from IPs/FQDNs known to be malicious — providing an additional automatic layer of protection.

questions.question: Which Azure Firewall feature LET'S a single, central firewall serve as a policy enforcement point for multiple VNets in a hub-and-spoke design?
options: Azure Firewall in a hub VNet, with spokes peered via VNet peering
A network security group on each subnet only
questions.explanation: Hub-and-spoke topology places the Azure Firewall in a hub VNet; spoke VNets peer to the hub and route traffic through the firewall via UDRs. This centralizes inspection and policy.

questions.question: Which TWO are differences between Azure Firewall and an NSG? (Select two.)
options: Azure Firewall supports FQDN filtering; NSGs do not
Azure Firewall is a managed service with central policy; NSGs are subnet/NIC-attached rule sets
NSGs are stateful but Azure Firewall is stateless
NSGs perform deep packet inspection like Azure Firewall
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall offers L7 application rules (FQDN), threat intel, and central policy across VNets. NSGs are stateful L3/L4 ACLs attached to a subnet or NIC. Both are stateful; only Azure Firewall has deeper L7 capability.

questions.question: What does an Azure Web Application Firewall (WAF) do?
options: Protects web applications from common Layer 7 exploits like SQL injection and cross-site scripting
questions.explanation: A WAF inspects HTTP/HTTPS traffic and blocks common application-layer attacks — OWASP Top 10 categories including SQLi, XSS, command injection, and more. Rule sets such as OWASP CRS are used as defaults.

questions.question: Which TWO Azure services can host an Azure Web Application Firewall? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Azure WAF can be deployed on Application Gateway (regional) or Azure Front Door (global). Azure CDN was a third historical option. Bastion and SQL are not WAF hosts.

questions.question: A WAF rule blocks a request containing patterns matching SQL injection. Which OWASP Top 10 category does this protect against?
questions.explanation: SQL injection falls under the Injection category in the OWASP Top 10. WAF rule sets like OWASP Core Rule Set detect injection patterns and block matching requests.

questions.question: WAF "Detection" mode and "Prevention" mode differ in which way?
options: Detection logs only; Prevention logs AND blocks matched requests
Detection encrypts traffic; Prevention does not
Detection requires premium SKU; Prevention is free
questions.explanation: In Detection mode the WAF evaluates and logs matches without blocking traffic — useful for tuning. Prevention mode applies the rule action (typically block) to matching requests.

questions.question: Which Azure service combines DDoS, WAF, and global load balancing in front of public web apps?
options: Azure Front Door (with WAF and Azure DDoS Protection)
questions.explanation: Azure Front Door provides global L7 load balancing and acceleration with integrated WAF; Azure DDoS Protection covers Front Door public endpoints. Together they form a strong perimeter for public web apps.

questions.question: What is the PRIMARY purpose of segmenting an Azure deployment into multiple virtual networks and subnets?
options: To control traffic flow, limit lateral movement, and apply policies at boundaries between workloads
To increase the cost of egress
To reduce the need for backups
questions.explanation: Network segmentation creates trust boundaries: traffic between segments is gated by NSGs, Azure Firewall, or peering rules. Segmentation limits the impact of compromise and enables tailored controls per zone.

questions.question: Which TWO constructs are commonly used to ISOLATE workloads in Azure? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: VNets and subnets define address space and broadcast domains; NSGs apply L3/L4 ACLs to subnets/NICs. Together they segment traffic. Email lists and cost tags are governance/operational tools, not network controls.

questions.question: Which feature of VNets lets you isolate critical workloads (e.g., production) from less-trusted ones (e.g., development) at the network layer?
options: Separate VNets and/or subnets, with NSGs or Azure Firewall between them
A single flat VNet with no rules
questions.explanation: Multi-VNet or multi-subnet topologies, combined with NSGs and an Azure Firewall, provide strong workload isolation. Flat networks and public endpoints increase lateral movement risk.

questions.question: What is a "hub-and-spoke" network topology in Azure?
options: A central VNet (hub) hosts shared services (firewall, ER/VPN gateway, DNS) and spoke VNets peer to it
A star-shaped storage account replication pattern
questions.explanation: Hub-and-spoke centralizes shared services in a hub VNet — connectivity (ExpressRoute/VPN), inspection (Azure Firewall), and DNS — while spoke VNets house workloads. It scales segmentation and policy.

questions.question: What is a network security group (NSG)?
options: A stateful Layer 3/4 access-control list applied to subnets or NICs that allows or denies traffic by source/destination IP, port, and protocol
A managed firewall replacing Azure Firewall
questions.explanation: An NSG is a basic, stateful filter (L3/L4 ACL) you attach to a subnet or NIC. Each NSG has inbound and outbound rules with priorities. NSGs do not perform deep inspection or FQDN filtering.

questions.question: Each NSG rule has a priority value. How does Azure evaluate multiple matching rules?
options: Lower priority numbers are evaluated first — once a rule matches, evaluation stops
Higher priority numbers are evaluated first
All rules are merged and any deny wins
questions.explanation: NSG rules are processed in priority order from lowest number (highest priority) to highest. The first rule that matches is applied; no further rules are evaluated for that traffic.

questions.question: Which TWO statements about NSGs are TRUE? (Select two.)
options: NSGs can be applied to a subnet AND a NIC simultaneously — both sets of rules are evaluated
NSGs perform Layer 7 inspection of HTTP traffic
NSGs include default rules that allow VNet-to-VNet and outbound internet traffic and deny inbound internet traffic
NSGs only operate on storage accounts
questions.explanation: NSGs can be attached at both subnet and NIC; both sets evaluate. Default rules permit intra-VNet, allow outbound internet, and deny inbound internet. NSGs do not perform L7 inspection or operate exclusively on storage.

questions.question: An admin needs to allow inbound traffic only from specific IP ranges representing partner networks. Which NSG capability supports this?
questions.explanation: NSGs match L3/L4 attributes including source IP/CIDR. FQDN-based filtering is an Azure Firewall feature, not an NSG capability.

questions.question: What is Azure Bastion?
options: A managed PaaS service that provides secure, browser-based RDP/SSH access to Azure VMs WITHOUT exposing public IPs on the VMs
questions.explanation: Azure Bastion deploys into your VNet and brokers RDP/SSH from the Azure portal over TLS. VMs need no public IP and no inbound RDP/SSH rule from the internet — drastically reducing attack surface.

questions.question: A company exposes RDP on a VM's public IP for admin access. Which SECURITY benefit does replacing this with Azure Bastion provide?
options: Eliminates the public RDP/SSH endpoint that is constantly scanned and brute-forced from the internet
Prevents the admin from using a browser
questions.explanation: Internet-exposed RDP/SSH ports are a top target for credential brute force and exploitation. Bastion eliminates the public endpoint, providing access through the Azure portal over TLS instead.

questions.question: Which TWO statements about Azure Bastion are TRUE? (Select two.)
options: Bastion is deployed inside a customer VNet (in an AzureBastionSubnet)
Bastion supports RDP and SSH connections through the Azure portal
Bastion requires a public IP on every VM
questions.explanation: Bastion lives in your VNet (specifically in a subnet named AzureBastionSubnet) and brokers RDP/SSH via the portal. It does not replace Azure Firewall and removes — rather than requires — public IPs on managed VMs.

questions.question: A user connects to a VM through Azure Bastion. From the VM's perspective, what is the source IP of the session?
options: The Bastion host's private IP within the VNet
questions.explanation: Bastion proxies the connection: the session arrives at the VM from Bastion's private IP inside the VNet. This simplifies NSG rules — allow RDP/SSH from Bastion's subnet rather than the internet.

questions.question: What is Azure Key Vault?
options: A managed service for securely storing and accessing secrets, keys, and certificates with auditing and access control
questions.explanation: Azure Key Vault centralizes management of cryptographic keys, secrets (passwords, connection strings), and TLS certificates. Access is governed by Entra-based authentication plus Key Vault access policies or Azure RBAC.

questions.question: Which TWO TYPES of objects can Key Vault store? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Key Vault stores keys (RSA/EC, optionally HSM-backed), secrets (arbitrary small text values), and certificates. Disks and audit logs are managed elsewhere (Disks, Azure Monitor).

questions.question: A developer wants to fetch a database password from Key Vault from an Azure Function WITHOUT storing any credentials in the function code. Which combination is RECOMMENDED?
options: Managed identity on the Function + RBAC/access policy on Key Vault granting "get secret"
A hardcoded Key Vault SAS token in the function code
A shared admin password for the Key Vault
A public anonymous endpoint on the Key Vault
questions.explanation: Best practice: assign the Function a managed identity, then grant that identity Get permission on the vault's secrets. The Function authenticates to Entra and reads the secret with no static credentials anywhere.

questions.question: Which TWO Key Vault tiers exist? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Key Vault offers Standard (software-protected keys) and Premium (HSM-backed keys validated to FIPS 140 standards). Azure Managed HSM is a separate, single-tenant HSM service for dedicated requirements.

questions.question: Which Key Vault FEATURE protects against accidental or malicious deletion by retaining deleted objects for a recoverable period?
questions.explanation: Soft delete retains deleted vaults and objects for a configurable period (default 90 days) so they can be recovered. Purge protection prevents purging during that window, defeating "delete and immediately purge" attacks.

questions.question: A company wants its Azure Storage data encrypted with a key it controls and rotates. Which capability is the BEST fit?
options: Customer-managed keys (CMK) stored in Azure Key Vault
questions.explanation: Storage Service Encryption can use customer-managed keys held in Key Vault — letting the customer manage rotation, access, and revocation while the platform performs encryption transparently.

questions.question: Which TWO ways to control access to Key Vault are SUPPORTED? (Select two.)
options: Azure RBAC at the vault scope
questions.explanation: Key Vault supports the legacy vault access policy model and modern Azure RBAC. Microsoft recommends Azure RBAC for new vaults — it integrates with PIM, conditional access, and standard role assignment tooling.

questions.question: A regulator requires keys to be created and used inside a FIPS 140-validated hardware security module. Which Microsoft service satisfies this?
options: Azure Key Vault Premium / Azure Managed HSM (HSM-backed keys)
Local file system on a VM
questions.explanation: HSM-backed keys in Key Vault Premium or Azure Managed HSM provide FIPS 140-validated key isolation in hardware. Keys can be generated inside the HSM and never exposed in plaintext outside it.

questions.question: What is Microsoft Defender for Cloud?
options: A cloud security posture management (CSPM) and cloud workload protection (CWP) platform for Azure, AWS, GCP, and on-premises resources
A SIEM service for hunting threats
questions.explanation: Microsoft Defender for Cloud is a unified CNAPP (Cloud-Native Application Protection Platform) combining CSPM (posture, recommendations, secure score) and CWP (workload-specific Defender plans). It covers multicloud and hybrid workloads.

questions.question: Which TWO categories of capability are provided by Microsoft Defender for Cloud? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Defender for Cloud delivers CSPM (continuous assessment, secure score, recommendations) and CWP via individual "Defender for X" plans (Servers, Storage, SQL, App Service, Containers, Key Vault, APIs, etc.).

questions.question: Secure Score in Microsoft Defender for Cloud is BEST described as:
options: A single number representing your current security posture, calculated from completed recommendations
Your billing total for the month
The number of users in your tenant
The number of subscriptions you own
questions.explanation: Secure Score aggregates the completion of Defender for Cloud recommendations into a percentage/number reflecting overall posture. Improving it directly translates to applying recommended security controls.

questions.question: Defender for Cloud recommendations are generated from which underlying capability?
options: Microsoft cloud security benchmark (MCSB) and other regulatory standards mapped into the service
questions.explanation: Defender for Cloud continuously evaluates resources against the Microsoft cloud security benchmark (MCSB) by default and can add other standards (CIS, PCI DSS, NIST, ISO 27001, etc.). Findings become actionable recommendations.

questions.question: Which TWO statements about Defender for Cloud are TRUE? (Select two.)
options: It supports multicloud — Azure, AWS, and GCP
Only Azure resources can be assessed
It can provide just-in-time VM access for inbound management ports
questions.explanation: Defender for Cloud supports Azure, AWS, and GCP via cloud connectors. Among its CWP features is just-in-time VM access — opening RDP/SSH for a specific user, IP, and time window. It does not replace identity services.

questions.question: Which Defender plan adds advanced threat protection to virtual machines and detects suspicious activities like brute force, fileless attacks, and crypto-mining?
questions.explanation: Defender for Servers extends Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (MDE) capabilities to Azure, AWS, GCP, and on-prem VMs — adding EDR, threat detection, vulnerability assessment, and adaptive application controls.

questions.question: Which Defender for Cloud plan would BEST detect malware uploaded to a blob storage account?
questions.explanation: Defender for Storage provides activity-based threat detection (anomalous access, suspicious patterns) and on-upload malware scanning for blob storage. It complements DLP/classification controls.

questions.question: Defender for Cloud's "Inventory" view shows what?
options: Resources across connected subscriptions and clouds, enriched with security posture (recommendations, alerts)
A list of all Entra ID users
questions.explanation: Inventory provides a single pane of glass over assets across Azure, AWS, GCP, and on-prem, decorated with security insights — counts of open recommendations, active alerts, and applicable Defender plans.

questions.question: Which TWO statements describe how Defender for Cloud can ONBOARD non-Azure resources? (Select two.)
options: Connect AWS / GCP accounts via cloud connectors
Install Azure Arc agents on on-prem servers
Move all resources into Azure first
questions.explanation: AWS and GCP connectors bring third-party cloud resources under Defender for Cloud. On-prem and other-cloud machines can also be onboarded via Azure Arc, which projects them as resources Defender can assess and protect.

questions.question: The Microsoft cloud security benchmark (MCSB) provides what?
options: Prescriptive best-practice controls and recommendations mapped to industry frameworks (CIS, NIST, PCI) used as the default standard in Defender for Cloud
A marketing comparison of cloud providers
questions.explanation: MCSB is Microsoft's prescriptive control framework for securing workloads in Azure (and multicloud). It is the default baseline in Defender for Cloud and is mapped to widely used standards.

questions.question: What does Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) do?
options: Continuously assesses cloud configuration against best practices and surfaces misconfigurations with prioritized recommendations
questions.explanation: CSPM continuously evaluates configuration and posture of cloud resources, surfacing misconfigurations, prioritizing risks, and tracking remediation. Defender for Cloud's foundational CSPM is free for Azure subscriptions.

questions.question: Which TWO tiers of CSPM does Microsoft Defender for Cloud offer? (Select two.)
questions.explanation: Foundational CSPM is included free and provides recommendations, secure score, and asset inventory. Defender CSPM (paid) adds advanced features like attack path analysis, agentless scanning, code-to-cloud insights, and DevOps security.

questions.question: Which advanced CSPM capability VISUALIZES how an attacker could chain misconfigurations to reach a critical asset?
questions.explanation: Attack path analysis identifies sequences of misconfigurations, exposures, and permissions that could let an attacker reach a sensitive asset. It is part of Defender CSPM and helps prioritize the most consequential fixes.

questions.question: A team wants automated recommendations on improving the posture of their AWS account in addition to Azure. Which Microsoft capability supports this?
options: Defender for Cloud with an AWS connector
questions.explanation: Defender for Cloud uses AWS and GCP connectors to extend CSPM (and selected workload protection plans) across clouds — providing unified posture management.

questions.question: How is Secure Score INCREASED in Defender for Cloud?
options: By implementing the security controls in recommendations across applicable resources
By increasing the number of subscriptions
questions.explanation: Each recommendation is associated with controls; completing them on more resources raises the score. The score reflects real posture improvement, not telemetry suppression.

questions.question: Which Azure service ENFORCES organizational rules (e.g., "all storage accounts must require HTTPS") and continuously evaluates resources for compliance?
questions.explanation: Azure Policy defines rules (policy definitions) and evaluates Azure resources for compliance. It can audit, deny non-compliant operations, or modify/deploy resources to remediate.

questions.question: In Defender for Cloud, what is a "security standard"?
options: A grouping of policies and controls (e.g., MCSB, PCI DSS, ISO 27001) used to assess and report on a workload's compliance posture
A type of Azure VM SKU
questions.explanation: Security standards in Defender for Cloud bundle the policies that constitute a benchmark (MCSB by default, plus optional frameworks like CIS, NIST, PCI). Compliance is calculated per standard and per regulatory control.

questions.question: Which TWO outcomes does applying the Microsoft cloud security benchmark (MCSB) to Azure subscriptions produce? (Select two.)
options: Recommendations that contribute to Secure Score
Mapped compliance reporting against industry frameworks
A list of expired domain names
questions.explanation: MCSB drives recommendations (which roll up to Secure Score) and provides traceability to industry frameworks (CIS, NIST, PCI), enabling compliance reporting from posture data.

questions.question: A company wants to BLOCK creation of storage accounts that allow public network access. Which Azure capability is BEST suited?
options: An Azure Policy with effect "Deny" enforcing the rule
A Defender for Endpoint EDR rule
A WAF rule on App Gateway
questions.explanation: Azure Policy with a Deny effect prevents non-compliant resource creation at the management plane. Conditional Access governs identity sign-ins; EDR and WAF protect endpoints/apps but do not block Azure resource configs.

questions.question: Which TWO benefits do security policies and standards in Defender for Cloud provide? (Select two.)
options: Consistent governance across many subscriptions and clouds
Continuous compliance monitoring with auditable evidence
questions.explanation: Standards-driven policies give a consistent control plane across cloud estates and continuous, auditable assessment. They are foundational to multicloud governance.

questions.question: What is Cloud Workload Protection (CWP) in Microsoft Defender for Cloud?
